timeline.heading: Lineage of your questions
timeline.subheading: A record of how your questions have been born, grown, and shifted

hero.lead: Oryzae is a writing app inspired by koji mold — Aspergillus oryzae. Set a question that matters to you, write into it for a week, and the system decomposes your text, surfaces a few essential snippets, and returns a single short letter. Slow self-dialogue, made on purpose.
hero.foot: Vertical or horizontal writing, in Japanese, English, and beyond.

snippet.1: As a nukadoko slowly absorbs the household it lives in, an AI may also change its character…

hero.question: YOUR QUESTION

concept.title: Brew your question while you write.

p1.title: Set a question
p1.body: Capture, in one sentence, the question that matters most right now. Everything else ferments around it.

p2.title: Write daily
p2.body: On a quiet vertical page, write whatever comes — typos, detours and all. The trace of your hand stays.

p3.title: A letter arrives
p3.body: Each week, a short letter and a few keywords distilled from your own words come back. Read them, then begin again.

process.lead: Inside Oryzae, three microbes — like the inhabitants of a nukadoko — each take a different role, and together brew a week of your words.

koji.output: Output: an analysis worksheet, a result diagram, snippets that illuminate the question.

lactic.body: Working from the worksheet and the snippets, this stage traces how your thinking has moved this week — not as a summary, but as a roughly 500-character poetic note in vocabulary deliberately offset from your own.

yeast.body: Three to five short phrases — "the autonomy of the dead", "the dead activated by the living" — that step a little back from your question and widen the imaginative space around it.

preview.title: Open the places.
preview.lead: Write daily in the Editor. Arrange your week on the Board. Look back on it from above in the Jar. At week's end, a Letter arrives. Four places, one slow practice.

editor.title: Add a title
editor.caption: Editor — a quiet vertical page. Optional typing effects — drifting, ghosting, fading characters — bring a small body to keystrokes.

jar.label: YOUR QUESTION · What is a design that lets nature inhabit the body?

letter.body: You have walked, this week, around the question of how the dead and the living might continue to live together. In a dream, the figure of Y arrives in ordinary clothes, then shrinks — quietly — back into the shape of a foetus and disappears. It is loss, and it is also a return to origin, a faint outline of a circular view of life and death. Across the week you begin to notice that an analytical posture may not be the right one toward what matters most. Forgetting, perhaps, is one form of living-with.
letter.caption: Letter — a 500-character letter, weekly. Not a summary; a small ember to keep you writing.

snippet.1: Forgetting, perhaps, is one form of living-with.
snippet.2: On nukadoko, people, and the way time mixes in.

entry.title: A design that feels the mountain

board.label: 7 cards · 5 entries · 2 snippets · 3 photos
board.caption: Board — A daily and weekly canvas where entries, snippets and photos sit beside each other. Memories begin to settle through relations, not through time.

2.title: Without immediacy
2.body: AI replies are deliberately delayed — once a week, like a letter. The exchange shifts from information processing to a quiet kind of relationship.

3.title: Fermentative time
3.body: Not an answer, a brewing. As a nukadoko takes on the flavour of the household it lives in, your thinking absorbs your own time and microbiome and slowly ripens.

4.title: Tech you can outgrow
4.body: The point is not dependence. Oryzae is meant to support, then quietly fall away as your own self-dialogue gets stronger.

lineage.label: LINEAGE
lineage.body: Oryzae carries forward what we learned from Pickles (2020–), a journaling tool for self-dialogue. The wider research context lives at ephemere.io.

1.q: How is this different from Pickles?
1.a: Pickles was a place to write the journal itself. Oryzae sets a question at the centre and adds the time around the writing — a fermentation process that returns your own words to you in a different shape.

2.q: Do I have to write vertically?
2.a: No. Vertical writing is the default, but you can switch to horizontal in one click. The font flips between mincho (serif) and gothic (sans) too.

3.a: Only the question you set, and the entries you tied to it during that week. Anything not tied to a question stays out of the fermentation.

4.q: Can I write in languages other than Japanese?
4.a: Yes — multilingual input is supported, including English. Vertical writing is tuned for Japanese and Chinese.

5.q: What stage is the project at?
5.a: Oryzae is a research preview. The shape of the app and its behaviour will continue to shift alongside the research.

step_concept.body: Plant a question on your mind, then water it with daily entries. Over time, an AI ferments what you've written and returns it as new insight.
step_concept.body_2: It works a little differently from a regular diary — let's walk through the four places that make it tick.

step_question.title: Plant your first question.
step_question.body: Try shaping what you're most preoccupied with into a short question. "Why…", "How might I…" — a sentence whose answer you don't have yet is plenty. You can edit it any time.
step_question.placeholder: e.g. Why am I bad at hurrying?
step_question.hint: It will be stored in the Jar — fermentation starts here.

step_editor.body_before_em: The editor is a quiet place to write. Put down whatever's on your mind. Select a sentence to lift it out as a
step_editor.body_after_em: — you can lay them out side by side later on the Board.

step_ferment.title: Let your questions rest in the Jar.
step_ferment.body: The entries you collect settle into the Jar and ferment on a schedule. The AI re-reads your words, finds patterns and shifts in your questions, and offers them back gently — that's the rhythm of Oryzae.
